title: Society in transition
description: Practice your skills with this exercise on postwar society in transition!

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Franklin Roosevelt’s rapid conversion from Constitutionalism to the doctrine of unlimited government, is an oft-told story. But I am here concerned not so much by the abandonment of States’ Rights by the national Democratic Party— an event that occurred some years ago when that party was captured by the Socialist ideologues in and about the labor movement— as by the unmistakable tendency of the Republican Party to adopt the same course. The result is that today *neither* of our two parties maintains a meaningful commitment to the principle of States’ Rights. Thus, the cornerstone of the Republic, our chief bulwark against the encroachment of individual freedom by Big Government, is fast disappearing under the piling sands of absolutism.” 

>-Source: Barry Goldwater, *The Conscience of a Conservative,* 1960

**Which of the following issues of the period was Goldwater most likely concerned with in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growing power of the federal government
the perceived decline in American culture and morals
the increase in federal spending after the Vietnam War
the spread of communism to countries in South America

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Franklin Roosevelt’s rapid conversion from Constitutionalism to the doctrine of unlimited government, is an oft-told story. But I am here concerned not so much by the abandonment of States’ Rights by the national Democratic Party— an event that occurred some years ago when that party was captured by the Socialist ideologues in and about the labor movement— as by the unmistakable tendency of the Republican Party to adopt the same course. The result is that today *neither* of our two parties maintains a meaningful commitment to the principle of States’ Rights. Thus, the cornerstone of the Republic, our chief bulwark against the encroachment of individual freedom by Big Government, is fast disappearing under the piling sands of absolutism.” 

>-Barry Goldwater, *The Conscience of a Conservative,* 1960

**The remarks in the excerpt were most likely given in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growth of the federal government’s powers
the increase in defense spending to fight the Vietnam War
the rapid relocation of people to states in the Sunbelt
the passage of legislation prohibiting poll taxes

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Franklin Roosevelt’s rapid conversion from Constitutionalism to the doctrine of unlimited government, is an oft-told story. But I am here concerned not so much by the abandonment of States’ Rights by the national Democratic Party— an event that occurred some years ago when that party was captured by the Socialist ideologues in and about the labor movement— as by the unmistakable tendency of the Republican Party to adopt the same course. The result is that today *neither* of our two parties maintains a meaningful commitment to the principle of States’ Rights. Thus, the cornerstone of the Republic, our chief bulwark against the encroachment of individual freedom by Big Government, is fast disappearing under the piling sands of absolutism.” 

>-Barry Goldwater, *The Conscience of a Conservative,* 1960

**Goldwater most likely wrote his account for which of the following reasons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to persuade the Republican Party to adopt conservative values
to convince federal investigators to search for Communists in the government
to advocate for an isolationist foreign policy in order to reduce defense spending
to encourage the federal government to push civil rights legislation forward

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Graph showing public trust in government, 1958 to 1980. The percentage of people who reported that they trusted the government peaked near 80% in 1964 and fell steadily until 1980.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d29e0d7eb418d3246c7ca86b53cac7c1.png)

Source: [Pew Research Center](https://www.people-press.org/2019/04/11/public-trust-in-government-1958-2019/), 2019.

**The overall trend from 1958  to 1980 depicted on the graph most directly reflects which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Americans lost confidence that the federal government could solve social and economic issues.
Americans relied heavily on the federal government to ease the conditions of poverty.
Americans feared the use of atomic weapons as the conflict with the Soviet Union escalated.
Americans showed support for the rise of liberalism in the Republican Party.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Graph showing public trust in government, 1958 to 1980. The percentage of people who reported that they trusted the government peaked near 80% in 1964 and fell steadily until 1980.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d29e0d7eb418d3246c7ca86b53cac7c1.png)

Source: [Pew Research Center](https://www.people-press.org/2019/04/11/public-trust-in-government-1958-2019/), 2019.

**Which of the following was a significant cause of the trend from 1958 to 1980 shown in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a series of political scandals within the federal government
a period of economic prosperity and reduction in unemployment
a lack of government support for social movements
a rise in conflicts between business leaders and labor activists

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Graph showing public trust in government, 1958 to 1980. The percentage of people who reported that they trusted the government peaked near 80% in 1964 and fell steadily until 1980.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d29e0d7eb418d3246c7ca86b53cac7c1.png)

Source: [Pew Research Center](https://www.people-press.org/2019/04/11/public-trust-in-government-1958-2019/), 2019.

**Which of the following factors contributed most to the major trend depicted in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a period of stagnation in economic growth and inflation of the dollar
a rise in third parties to advocate for farmers’ rights and fight corruption
an expansion of the voting-eligible population with the ratification of the Twenty-sixth Amendment
an end to the global conflict with the Soviet Union after several notable losses in South America

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“To propose federal ‘intrusion’ into the sanctity of the state-local-private preserve of education was to stride boldly into a uniquely dangerous political [minefield] that pitted Democrat against Republican; liberal against conservative; Catholic against Protestant and Jew; federal power against states rights; white against black; and rich constituency against poor in mercurial cross-cutting alliances.” 

>-Source: Hugh Davis Graham, historian, *The Uncertain Triumph: Federal Education Policy in the Kennedy and Johnson Years,* 1984

**The developments described in the excerpt best illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growing clashes between political groups over the power of the federal government
the rise in debates over continued American intervention in the Vietnam War
the passage of a provision in the Civil Rights Act of 1964 that increased opportunities for women
the loss in public confidence in the federal government’s ability to solve social and economic problems

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“To propose federal ‘intrusion’ into the sanctity of the state-local-private preserve of education was to stride boldly into a uniquely dangerous political [minefield] that pitted Democrat against Republican; liberal against conservative; Catholic against Protestant and Jew; federal power against states rights; white against black; and rich constituency against poor in mercurial cross-cutting alliances.” 

>-Source: Hugh Davis Graham, historian, *The Uncertain Triumph: Federal Education Policy in the Kennedy and Johnson Years,* 1984

**The pattern described in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following long-term developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the realignment of political parties over the role of the federal government
the rise of social movements to address inequalities in American society
the decline in public trust in the federal government because of political scandals
the deregulation of several government programs to reduce federal spending

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“To propose federal ‘intrusion’ into the sanctity of the state-local-private preserve of education was to stride boldly into a uniquely dangerous political [minefield] that pitted Democrat against Republican; liberal against conservative; Catholic against Protestant and Jew; federal power against states rights; white against black; and rich constituency against poor in mercurial cross-cutting alliances.” 

>-Source: Hugh Davis Graham, historian, *The Uncertain Triumph: Federal Education Policy in the Kennedy and Johnson Years,* 1984

**The developments described in the excerpt most directly reflect which of the following changes in the 1960s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in conflicts between conservatives and liberals over the role of government
the extension of voting rights to people aged eighteen to twenty-one years old
the spread in support for a sexual revolution that appealed to the younger generation
the increase in the number of troops sent to Vietnam to fight communism

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“. . . [In] the late 1960s, conservative Protestants succeeded not only in making alliances with Republican politicians, but in changing the agenda of the party. . . . [This] time, they focused more on the culture wars than the Cold War. Conservative Protestants who mobilized against feminism, abortion, pornography, and gay rights acquired control of the Republican Party, partly because of their long-standing alliances with Republican politicians, but perhaps more important because of the united front they presented, and because of demographic and political shifts that favored evangelicals. By the beginning of the twenty-first century, the Christian Right was the most powerful group in the GOP.”

>-Source: Daniel K. Williams, historian, *God’s Own Party: The Making of the Christian Right,* 2012

**The developments described in the excerpt best illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the influence of evangelical groups on politics
the impact of the Twenty-seventh Amendment
the rise of liberalism within the Republican Party
the growth of the federal government’s power

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“. . . [In] the late 1960s, conservative Protestants succeeded not only in making alliances with Republican politicians, but in changing the agenda of the party. . . . [This] time, they focused more on the culture wars than the Cold War. Conservative Protestants who mobilized against feminism, abortion, pornography, and gay rights acquired control of the Republican Party, partly because of their long-standing alliances with Republican politicians, but perhaps more important because of the united front they presented, and because of demographic and political shifts that favored evangelicals. By the beginning of the twenty-first century, the Christian Right was the most powerful group in the GOP.”

>-Source: Daniel K. Williams, historian, *God’s Own Party: The Making of the Christian Right,* 2012

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emergence of a sexual revolution that challenged traditional values
the continued presence of American troops in southern Vietnam
the threat of the Soviet Union’s nuclear warfare capabilities
the rise of Jimmy Carter to the presidency after several political scandals

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“. . . [In] the late 1960s, conservative Protestants succeeded not only in making alliances with Republican politicians, but in changing the agenda of the party. . . . [This] time, they focused more on the culture wars than the Cold War. Conservative Protestants who mobilized against feminism, abortion, pornography, and gay rights acquired control of the Republican Party, partly because of their long-standing alliances with Republican politicians, but perhaps more important because of the united front they presented, and because of demographic and political shifts that favored evangelicals. By the beginning of the twenty-first century, the Christian Right was the most powerful group in the GOP.”

>-Daniel K. Williams, historian, *God’s Own Party: The Making of the Christian Right,* 2012

**One major change in United States politics from 1960 to 1980  was the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: emergence of a political action group that sought to further a religious agenda.
establishment of poll taxes to limit African American voting.
deregulation of several government agencies to reduce the size of the government.
buildup of nuclear and conventional weapons to combat the Soviet Union.
